issue_title: Dataset names are not always unique
issue_body:
Occasionally dataset names will not be unique. Here is screenshot for proof:
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3702067/15973727/c78ed802-2ef9-11e6-897d-f445db29fe58.png)

Notice the dataset name, `PyGraphistry/8XGYH6K9G1` appears twice. 

We believe the problem to be the random_string function in util.py not generating proper hashes. 

(For further discussion, we should consider using content based hashes for caching reasons. We could then avoid reuploading datasets which have already been uploaded. This should be wrapped into the design of the REST API).

issue_title: Python 3 support
issue_body: Currently we only support Python 2. In future releases we want to support both Python 2 and 3. [This](https://docs.python.org/3/howto/pyporting.html) howto outlines the main steps towards Python 3 support.

issue_title: create an option to disable menu installation in Windows OS
issue_body:
related to #1717 and #2136
Please create an option or flag to disable menu installation upon "conda create" in the Windows OS. As mentioned in the related issues, this will give an option to circumvent the PermissionError problems in Windows when creating a new conda environment in an admin installation as a non-admin on Windows.

rationale (may be TL;DR):
People that care about this, usually have a use case where they want to be flexible installing packages as non-admins (because for example going via the admin has too much lead time) but still want to offer a system-wide miniconda system with the environments centralized to users that don't know or care about how to create environments but want these environments maintained system-wide.

issue_title: conda create --clone creates broken env when crossing filesystems
issue_body:
The conda create subcommand attempts to create hard links from source (non-symlink) files in the cloned env to the destination env.  However, when source and destination directories are on different filesystems, most commonly in an NFS environment, creation of those hard links fails.  The resulting environment has many broken links to shared libraries, as the actual shared library contents never get copied.

Creation of the hard links is attempted in conda.install._link when link type is LINK_HARD.  The underlying call to os.link throws an exception, but the exception is caught by the install.link() function and logged somewhere.  No error message gets written to the console.

The use of the --copy option in conda create does not change the behavior: hard links are still attempted, but silently fail.

I suggest either (1) detecting cross-filesysystem clones and copy files in that case instead of trying to hard link them; or (2) make --copy change the behavior of conda.install._link to use LINK_COPY.

In addition, the catching of the OSError exception in conda.install.link seems misguided.  Failure to create a link means the created env is in all likelihood broken.  The install should just quit and report the error on the console, and ensure that the script exits nonzero.

issue_title: `conda build .` deletes scripts in "bin" folder
issue_body:
When building a simple conda package with 
```conda build .```
it ends up deleting any scripts mentioned in the scripts-argument of the setup function in setup.py.

Here is an example setup.py file which causes the issue:

```python
from setuptools import setup
import os

from setuptools import setup, find_packages

long_description = open("README.md").read()

entry_points = None

install_requires = []

setup(
    name="Package name",
    packages=find_packages(),
    entry_points=entry_points,
    include_package_data=True,
    scripts=["bin/mypackage"]
)
```

The corresponding meta.yaml file looks as follows:
```yaml
package:
  name: mypackage
  version: 0.1
  
source:
  path: .
 
build:
    script: python setup.py install

requirements:
  build:
    - python
    - setuptools
    - pip

  run:
    - python
    - setuptools
    - pip
```

After running 
```conda build .```
the bin/mypackage file has been deleted.

This does not happen if the build command is issued a directory above the source directory, i.e. 
```conda build mypackage```

issue_title: Activate issue with parallel activations - "File exists" in symlinked path
issue_body:
I'm working in a cluster environment where I am trying to activate a conda environment in multiple parallel processes. After recently upgrading to the latest release (4.1.2), I've been receiving a "File exists" error in some of those processes, similar to the below:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/symln_home/anaconda/bin/conda", line 6, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/Users/tomflem/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/main.py", line 48, in main
    activate.main()
  File "/Users/tomflem/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/activate.py", line 163, in main
    conda.install.symlink_conda(prefix, root_dir, shell)
  File "/Users/tomflem/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/install.py", line 582, in symlink_conda
    symlink_conda_hlp(prefix, root_dir, where, symlink_fn)
  File "/Users/tomflem/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/install.py", line 599, in symlink_conda_hlp
    symlink_fn(root_file, prefix_file)
OSError: [Errno 17] File exists
```

I've been able to repro this issue on a laptop when my PATH to the root anaconda install contains a symlinked directory ("symln_home," in this example). Doesn't seem to be a problem when the PATH is symlink-free. The below has produced the error consistently for me on OS X:

```
$ echo $PATH
/symln_home/anaconda/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin
```

```python
from multiprocessing import Pool
import os

call = ['source activate myenv && echo $PATH' for i in range(30)]
pool = Pool(4)
pool.map(os.system, call)
pool.close()
```

Is this a bug, or are there any workarounds for this particular use case? In my cluster environment, my HOME directory (where I've installed anaconda) is symlinked, which I would assume is a fairly common setup in shared computing (I don't admin it, so unfortunately I can't change that aspect).

issue_title: put_template() is not working as expected
issue_body:
Hello,

Following the official documentation on [indice template](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/indices-templates.html) I wanted to use the method `put_template` to add an index template. But the method constructs the API call to `PUT /_search/template/{id_of_template}` [code here](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/master/elasticsearch/client/__init__.py#L1323) which doesn't seem to work on elasticsearch 6
I corrected the code with the correct API call and it's successfully creating a template index : 
```python
return self.transport.perform_request('PUT', _make_path('template', id), params=params, body=body)
```
Was I right to correct the method or should I use another method to create index template ?

Best regards

issue_title: Certificate error with version of requests

issue_title: vm run-command --script not working on Linux VMs
issue_body:
When trying to use run-command with a file as the argument against a linux target it is consistently failing. On inspection of the file some odd escape characters are being inserted when it copies the file on to the VM for execution:

The documentation mentions:
```
--scripts
Script lines separated by whites spaces. Use @{file} to load from a file.
```

I can't seem to figure out what its wanting me to pass this file as to make it run correctly. Its uploading to the server but inserting the odd escape characters as seen below. Didn't seen any documentation on what to do here.

### Local file:
```
# cat -v test.sh 
logger "test"
```
### Command Executed to run against vm and failure message:
```
bash-4.3# az vm run-command invoke --resource-group 'TEST' --name 'TEST1' --command-id 'RunShellScript' --scripts @ ./test.sh --verbose
Deployment failed. Correlation ID: -------. VM has reported a failure when processing extension 'RunCommandLinux'. Error message: "Enable failed: failed to execute command: command terminated with exit status=126
[stdout]

[stderr]
/bin/sh: /var/lib/waagent/run-command/download/6/script.sh: cannot execute binary file
".
```

### File on the server with odd characters:
```
# cat -v /var/lib/waagent/run-command/download/6/script.sh
l^@o^@g^@g^@e^@r^@ ^@"^@t^@e^@s^@t^@"^@
```
Tried running this with multiple iterations, single lines, multiple. Declaratives.

I thought for a bit @{} was referencing a hashmap I.E. powershell but trying different variations of loading the file in a array didn't produce any tangible results.

It almost looks like it is compressing the file on the way up and incorrectly uncompressing it.

---

### Environment summary

Install Method Docker / Arch / Bash 

When trying to run az vm run-command invoke --scripts the text in our script file seems to be getting converted and having odd characters inserted?

issue_title: View Logs for Azure App Service and Functions
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
When developers are developing Web apps and functions, they often need to view server side logs. They are forced to jump out of their dev environments and view logs by navigating through multiple blades in Azure Portal. This must be easier than that.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
```
$ az webapp viewlogs -g MyResourceGroup -n MyUniqueAppName --last 500
$ az functionapp viewlogs -g MyResourceGroup -n MyUniqueAppName --last 500
```
Developers can view logs without navigating through Azure Portal

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
8 Azure Portal clicks to view logs

issue_title: SSL upload, bind, unbind commands broken when a web app and its service plan are in different resource groups
issue_body:
### Environment summary

**Install Method:** How did you install the CLI? (e.g. pip, interactive script, apt-get, Docker, MSI, nightly)  
Interactive
**CLI Version:** What version of the CLI and modules are installed? (Available with `az --version`)  
2.0.2
**OS Version:** What OS and version are you using?  
Mac OSX 10.11.6
**Shell Type:** What shell are you using? (e.g. bash, cmd.exe, PowerShell)  
Bash
### Description
When an app service plan is in resource group A and the web app is in resource group B, the `az appservice web config ssl bind|unbind|upload` commands will not work as expected. You'll be able to `upload`, but not `bind` or `unbind` any certs. 

Judging from how the Azure Portal behaves, the commands should be using the resource group of the app service plan rather than the web app for storing/retrieving the cert, e.g. https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/2793bcddf8876661227d3fb3efd34f105b79f8a3/src/command_modules/azure-cli-appservice/azure/cli/command_modules/appservice/custom.py#L851 should look more like
```
def upload_ssl_cert(resource_group_name, name, certificate_password, certificate_file):
    client = web_client_factory()
    webapp = _generic_site_operation(resource_group_name, name, 'get')
    cert_resource_group_name = _get_resource_group_name_by_resource_id(webapp.server_farm_id)
    cert_file = open(certificate_file, 'rb')
    cert_contents = cert_file.read()
    hosting_environment_profile_param = webapp.hosting_environment_profile
    if hosting_environment_profile_param is None:
        hosting_environment_profile_param = ""

    thumb_print = _get_cert(certificate_password, certificate_file)
    cert_name = _generate_cert_name(thumb_print, hosting_environment_profile_param,
                                    webapp.location, cert_resource_group_name)
    cert = Certificate(password=certificate_password, pfx_blob=cert_contents,
                       location=webapp.location)
    return client.certificates.create_or_update(cert_resource_group_name, cert_name, cert)

def _get_resource_group_name_by_resource_id(resource_id):
    '''Returns the resource group name from parsing the resource id.
    :param str resource_id: The resource id
    '''
    resource_id = resource_id.lower()
    resource_group_keyword = '/resourcegroups/'
    return resource_id[resource_id.index(resource_group_keyword) + len(resource_group_keyword):
                       resource_id.index('/providers/')]
```

issue_body:
Currently there is no way to figure out which data centers are able to create acs clusters. The only way to know right now as far as I know is to fail through trial-by-error, then use the regions listed in the error output. For example:

```
><> az acs create --resource-group="${AZURE_RG_NAME}" --location="canadaeast" --orchestrator-type=kubernetes --master-count=1 --agent-count=1 --agent-vm-size="Standard_D2_v2" --admin-username="k8sadmin" --name="${AZURE_SERVICE_NAME}" --dns-prefix="${AZURE_DNS_PREFIX}" --ssh-key-value @/home/bacongobbler/.ssh/id_rsa.pub 
waiting for AAD role to propagate.done
The provided location 'canadaeast' is not available for resource type 'Microsoft.ContainerService/containerServices'. List of available regions for the resource type is 'japaneast,centralus,eastus2,japanwest,eastasia,southcentralus,australiaeast,australiasoutheast,brazilsouth,southeastasia,westus,northcentralus,westeurope,northeurope,eastus'.
```

It would be great if there was a way to query which regions are available for the given resource type before creating the cluster. Right now it's a matter of picking one from `az account list-locations --query [].name --output tsv` and hoping it works.

issue_title: How can I modify the VM list of the backend pools of load balancers?
issue_body:
I have a load balancer configure in Azure (ARM) and I have 2 back end pools: prod, stage. Through the GUI, when I want to promote a staging server to production, I remove it from the stage pool and place it in the prod pool. I'm very confused about how this all works because when I provision the stack, I first provision the load balancer and when I provision the NIC that the VM will attach to, I place that NIC in the backend pool I want. However, when I want to move the VM to another pool, I no longer do that at the NIC level. I have to do that at the load balancer. 

Using the Python SDK, I am able to see which NIC is in the backend pool if I query the LB but there seems to be no way to modify that. I can also query the NIC and see which backend pool it's associated with but again, no way to modify (from what I can tell). This is what I have so far:

    # Create the client
    network_client = azure.mgmt.network.NetworkResourceProviderClient(creds)

    # Get all LBs
    lbs = network_client.load_balancers

    # select LB in question
    lb = lbs.get(group,'cc-lb01')

    # get all pools
    pools = lb.load_balancer.backend_address_pools

    # set variables for pools
    prod = pools[0]
    stage = pools[1]
    
The output of print(dir(stage)) is:

It returns an array of objects:

There is only 1 item in that array so I set that item to a variable:

    beip = stage.backend_ip_configurations[0].id

and when I see what my options are with 'beip', this is where I dead end. 

    /subscriptions/xxx-xxx-xxx/resourceGroups/myresourcegroup/providers/Microsoft.Network/networkInterfaces/app-green04-nic/ipConfigurations/ipconfig1

The output of print(dir(beip)) is:

issue_title: Incorrect requirement for azure.mgmt.compute
issue_body:
It seems that the version requirement for `azure-common~=1.1` in `azure.mgmt.compute` is too broad: new versions of the package reference `azure.profiles`, which was introduced in `azure-common` 1.1.9.

For example, if I try to install the latest version (4.0.0rc2) of `azure.mgmt.common` in an environment which already has a previous, older version of `azure-common`, the installation completes successfully but I can't import anything from the new package.

Steps to reproduce:
1. From a clean environment, install an older version of `azure-common`, then install the latest `azure.mgmt.compute` from source.
```bash
pip install "azure-common<=1.1.8"
git clone https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python.git
pip install azure-sdk-for-python/azure-mgmt-compute
```

2. In a Python console, import the new package.
```python
>>> import azure.mgmt.compute
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/ageorgou/anaconda/envs/test-azure/lib/python3.6/site-packages/azure/mgmt/compute/__init__.py", line 8, in <module>
    from .compute_management_client import ComputeManagementClient
  File "/Users/ageorgou/anaconda/envs/test-azure/lib/python3.6/site-packages/azure/mgmt/compute/compute_management_client.py", line 16, in <module>
    from azure.profiles import KnownProfiles, ProfileDefinition
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'azure.profiles'
```

Expected behaviour is for the import to work, as it successfully does with newer (>=1.1.9) versions of `azure-common`.

issue_title: Come up with a better solution for building docs
issue_body: Docs that are in master should be just the manually written content, with nothing generated (i.e., IPython notebooks with *no* output from the cells). We should actually compile the docs and commit the output to a different branch, and maybe we can set up something on Travis to do this automatically every time there is a commit to master. This will help to minimize merge conflicts and also remove the problem of having to remember to regenerate the docs all the time.

issue_title: Use "requirejs" instead of "require"
issue_body:
Wishlist item: some time ago I worked on a desktop wrapper for iPython - https://github.com/mangecoeur/ipython-desktop . One problem I ran into was that I embed the page into a nodeJs based desktop container (originally NodeWebkit, since shifted to github's Atom runtime). Node uses "require" to load modules, but this require is totally different from the AMD "requirejs" system. When you try to blend the browser and server code you get a name conflict which in theory could be fixed with some monkey patching, but in practice it's so flaky I never got it to work properly.

However, requirejs allows you to use `requirejs` function instead of `require`. From a quick overview of the code, it seems the root "require" function is only used in a few places - changing these might make the notebook more friendly to interact with from nodejs programs.

issue_title: Notebook page broken in master?
exists() method for Checkpoints class
issue_body:
Presently the [`Checkpoint`](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/3.x/IPython/html/services/contents/checkpoints.py#L13) class has `list_checkpoints()` method that is being used to verify if a check point exist for a given file or not, such as [here](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blame/3.x/IPython/html/services/contents/filemanager.py#L387):
````
                # One checkpoint should always exist for notebooks.
                if not self.checkpoints.list_checkpoints(path):
                    self.create_checkpoint(path)
````
In some scenarios listing all checkpoints is too costly affair when we are just interested in only checking the existence of atleast one.

Perhaps the `Checkpoint` class should have `checkpoint_exists_for(path)` kind of mechanism.

(PS: Originally posted [here](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/8523))

issue_title: subclassed models are not serialized
issue_body:
Consider the following:

```
from schematics.models import Model
from schematics.types import StringType
from schematics.types.compound import ModelType

class Asset(Model):
    file_name = StringType()

class S3Asset(Asset):
    bucket_name = StringType()

class Product(Model):
    title = StringType()
    asset = ModelType(Asset)
    
asset = S3Asset({'bucket_name': 'assets_bucket', 'file_name': 'bar'})

product = Product({'title': 'baz', 'asset': asset})
print product.to_native()
```

This code outputs: `{'asset': {'storage_location': u's3'}, 'title': u'baz'}`

Because "Product.asset" is defined as an "Asset", rather than an S3Asset, schematics does not serialize the S3Asset-specific fields.

I believe this is because the list of fields to be serialized is defined in ModelMeta, before the model "knows" that it is being passed a subclass.

I'm not yet sure of how I'd fix this - perhaps update `to_primitive` to do self inspection there, rather than ModelMeta and using `self.__class__`?

If you add a pattern to the `regex=` argument for `StringType()` when you subclass the module Python will error with:

```
TypeError: Error when calling the metaclass bases
    cannot deepcopy this pattern object
```

Since `validate_regex()` isn't called in a tight loop and Python internally caches patterns I think just storing the string instead of the compiled pattern is a smart way to go.

This change will enable deepcopy to work and enable subclassing of models with `StringType(regex=...)` to work. Also the feels a little like premature optimization anyway.

PR #321

issue_title: Improve flexible handling of datetime representation
issue_body:
Schematics does not allow efficient and flexible handling of datetime representation.

**`DateTimeType` does not allow parsing from timestamp**

```python
In [3]: DateTimeType().to_native(1426494600)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ConversionError                           Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-3-da60245683cd> in <module>()
----> 1 DateTimeType().to_native(1426494600)

/opt/Personal/Dropbox/Code/schematics/schematics/types/base.py in to_native(self, value, context)
    727             except (ValueError, TypeError):
    728                 continue
--> 729         raise ConversionError(self.messages['parse'].format(value))
    730
    731     def to_primitive(self, value, context=None):

ConversionError: [u'Could not parse 1426494600. Should be ISO8601.']
```

That's annoying because deserializing should be permissive, especially when multiple input format can be used. This is what #288 aims to fix.

**`TimeStampType` has multiple problems**

It does not override `to_native`, which means that it's basically useless and returns the same error as `DateTimeType` (reported in #271 as well):

```python
In [1]: from schematics.types.temporal import TimeStampType

In [2]: TimeStampType().to_native(1426494600)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ConversionError                           Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-2-72f40cf29e6e> in <module>()
----> 1 TimeStampType().to_native(1426494600)

/opt/Personal/Dropbox/Code/schematics/schematics/types/base.pyc in to_native(self, value, context)
    727             except (ValueError, TypeError):
    728                 continue
--> 729         raise ConversionError(self.messages['parse'].format(value))
    730
    731     def to_primitive(self, value, context=None):

ConversionError: [u'Could not parse 1426494600. Should be ISO8601.']
```

The more important problem is that it ties a type to its deserialization format. We should decouple serialization/deserialization and the type here. 

Let's say I expect a date & time to be deserialized from either timestamp or iso8601 format, and serialized in iso8601. Neither `TimeStampType` nor `DateTimeType` fits the bill here, because:

* `DateTimeType` does not parse timestamps
* `TimeStampType` (assuming we fix it `to_native` method, I have a fix for it as well) only serializes timestamps.

I think the proper fix would be to have `DateTimeType` parse timestamps.

issue_title: Add option for overriding job_conf.xml.
Setting TMPDIR not respected when > 11 characters long.
issue_body:
Please change the code in planemo/galaxy/config.py, in def _config_directory(ctx, **kwds):

On our galaxy, our jobs are submitted to a cluster to run. To test using planemo, temporary files must be in a location that is visible from the cluster. The smallest length of such directory locations that are writable by our users are either "/N/dc2/scratch/username" or "/N/u/username", and usernames are quite frequently longer than 5 or 6 characters long.

The temporary fix that is referenced in https://github.com/galaxyproject/planemo/pull/460

causes any value of TMPDIR that is longer than 10 characters to be ignored and the value is set to /tmp instead. That will not work in a cluster situation, because the /tmp where galaxy runs is a totally different file system than the /tmp on the cluster nodes (each node has its own /tmp). Even though the test is for 20 characters, before the test, a 9 character directory name (plus a "/" separator) is added to the TMPDIR variable when calling mkdtemp() to create the config_directory value. 

There needs to be a way that we can set TMPDIR and make that value be respected.
Since the issue with long names is with conda, then some sort of test could be made as to whether conda is being used, and if the name is too long and conda is being used, then a message about the situation could be printed for the user to find a work around for their system, rather than assuming that /tmp is going to work for them.

Another solution might be to add a command line argument that would be used to set the config_directory variable.

Thanks,
Cicada

issue_title: Assumption of resizing need/success if PIL module is available
issue_body:
The client uses the importability of `PIL` as an indicator that it "can resize":

https://github.com/Clarifai/clarifai-python/blob/c411cc53261d41f3b9c7031223f4ea5386690dfc/clarifai/client/client.py#L7-L8

However, it uses "can resize" as an unconditional/non-overridable "should resize" here:

https://github.com/Clarifai/clarifai-python/blob/c411cc53261d41f3b9c7031223f4ea5386690dfc/clarifai/client/client.py#L537-L540

Alas, the underlying resizing that's attempted via `PIL` is totally broken if the files that are passed in are `file` objects encapsulating non-randomly-seekable pipes (which the module is perfectly capable of using otherwise). For applications where the resizing is dealt with externally, and the image data piped into the client, the unconditional nature of this additional resizing becomes a show-stopper.

It is especially surprising when previously-working code stops working merely due to the independent installation of `PIL` via `pip` for an entirely different purpose. As a workaround, doing:
```
import sys
sys.modules['PIL'] = None
```
_before_ this client module is imported "hides" `PIL` from it.

It would be very desirable to disable additional resizing via an explicit argument, probably to the constructor.

issue_title: 'flat' feeds are not configured

issue_title: Warnings from flake8 due to `visitor`
issue_body:
With flake8 3.5.0 and flake8-bugbear installed, I get the following messages spammed all over my test logs (when using pytest-flake8):

```
WARNING:flake8.processor:Plugin requested optional parameter "visitor" but this is not an available parameter.
```

This is because `visitor` is an `attr.ib`, which gets added automatically to `__init__`. Apparently, flake8 [inspects](http://flake8.pycqa.org/en/latest/plugin-development/plugin-parameters.html#indicating-desired-data) and [validates](http://flake8.pycqa.org/en/latest/internal/utils.html#flake8.utils.parameters_for) all constructor parameters.

issue_title: Use plugin-based design for field definitions
issue_body:
NailGun contains numerous bits of code like this:

``` python
        if _get_version(server_config) >= Version('6.1.1'):  # default: True
            self._fields.update({
                'default_content_view': entity_fields.OneToOneField(
                    ContentView
                ),
                'library': entity_fields.OneToOneField(LifecycleEnvironment),
            })
```

These bits of code are necessary because NailGun is (somewhat) intolerant of fields being declared incorrectly.

<ins>[edit: The following two examples are neat, but you can skip down to the "this is tiresome" section without missing too much.]</ins>


There's at least two especially obvious ways in which this is true. One way to trigger an exception is to pass in something silly when instantiating an entity:

``` python
>>> from nailgun import entities
>>> entities.Organization(not_a_real_field='foo')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "nailgun/entities.py", line 2586, in __init__
    super(Organization, self).__init__(server_config, **kwargs)
  File "nailgun/entity_mixins.py", line 387, in __init__
    .format(self._fields.keys(), kwargs.keys())
nailgun.entity_mixins.NoSuchFieldError: Valid fields are ['domain', 'description', 'default_content_view', 'library', 'compute_resource', 'id', 'subnet', 'realm', 'name', 'title', 'smart_proxy', 'hostgroup', 'label', 'environment', 'config_template', 'media', 'user'], but received ['not_a_real_field'] instead.
```

This is done to prevent mis-use of an entity by and end user, and it's sane behaviour. Another way to trigger an exception is for Satellite to not hand back enough data. That's how we end up with work-arounds like this:

``` python
    def create(self, create_missing=None):
        """Do extra work to fetch a complete set of attributes for this entity.

        For more information, see `Bugzilla #1230873
        <https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1230873>`_.

        """
        return Organization(
            self._server_config,
            id=self.create_json(create_missing)['id'],
        ).read()
```

This is tiresome. As time goes on, we're going to add more and more conditional field definitions into every single entity. This will bloat the entity definitions and make them harder to maintain as time goes on. We should find a better solution.

One possibility is to use a plug-in based architecture. We currently have this layout:

```
$ tree nailgun
nailgun
├── client.py
├── config.py
├── entities.py
├── entity_fields.py
├── entity_mixins.py
└── __init__.py

0 directories, 6 files
```

Imagine that we added schema definitions, like so:

```
$ tree nailgun/
nailgun/
├── client.py
├── config.py
├── entities.py
├── entity_fields.py
├── entity_mixins.py
├── __init__.py
└── schemas
    ├── foreman
    │   └── 1.7.2.py
    └── satellite
        ├── 6.0.8.py
        ├── 6.1.0.py
        └── 6.1.1.py

3 directories, 10 files
```

The schemas could also be JSON or some other format, but the most straightforward approach would probably be for each schema to be a Python file containing field definitons just like what we have now. When an entity is instantiated, it can pick out the right schema and populate its `_meta` and `_fields` attributes.

Doing this would let us drop a huge amount of code (both magic constants and conditionals) from `nailgun/entities.py`, and we would be able to properly support Satellite, Foreman, RHCI and so on.

issue_title: If kernel subdirectory doesn't exist?
issue_body:
I think this is correct (was actually using a kernel based on metakernel, but may also be the case with metakernel_python etc), probably an IPython bug:

Couldn't install into the system kernel directory until it existed. I was able to force it to be created with:
```
sudo ipython kernelspec install-self
```
That makes the Python3 kernel explicit on disk, and forced the creation of `/usr/local/share/jupyter/kernels/`

I was then able to pip3 install `calysto_scheme` -U again, and it did install it this time.

issue_title: Parsing isoformat sometimes gets confused between single digit years and months
issue_body:
The date and time "0005-01-01T00:00:05" (which is output from the standard library's isoformat method) gets parsed incorrectly:

```pycon
>>> from dateutil.parser import parse
>>> parse('0005-01-01T00:00:05').isoformat()
'0001-05-01T00:00:05
```
The month and the year have been swapped here, despite the year being in an unambiguous four digit form.

Curiously, the trailing 5 seconds are *not* an accidental detail and are required to reproduce the bug. If it is changed to zero seconds then the problem goes away:

```pycon
>>> parse('0005-01-01T00:00:00').isoformat()
'0005-01-01T00:00:00'
```

issue_title: locale_dir should default to 'locales'
issue_body:
The default value of `locale_dirs` is `['locales']` since Sphinx 1.5.
Therefore, for consistency and convenience, the name of default output directory of sphinx-intl should be a 'locales' directory.

issue_title: dump(obj, many=None, update_fields=True, **kwargs) throws error when **kwargs included
issue_body:
I noticed the **kwargs in the argument list fo dump(). Here is the API documentation:

    dump(obj, many=None, update_fields=True, **kwargs)
        Serialize an object to native Python data types according to this Schema’s fields.

        Parameters:	
          obj – The object to serialize.
          many (bool) – Whether to serialize obj as a collection. If `None`, the value for self.many is used.
          update_fields (bool) – Whether to update the schema’s field classes. Typically set to `True`, 
            but may be `False` when serializing a homogenous collection. This parameter is used by `fields.Nested` to avoid multiple updates.
          Returns: A tuple of the form (`data`, `errors`)
          Return type: `MarshalResult, a collections.namedtuple`

The documentation doesn't mention what **kwargs are used for, and I thought that I might be able to get some data in to my @post_dump function by using it. So I started to play around with a simple example:

    dump(my_obj, many=None, update_fields=True, txn=1)

I received the following error:

    File "/home/apeters/git/donate_0/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/marshmallow/schema.py", line 513, in dump
        **kwargs
    TypeError: serialize() got an unexpected keyword argument 'txn'

Looking at schema.py at line 513:

    preresult = self._marshal(
                    processed_obj,
                    self.fields,
                    many=many,
                    # TODO: Remove self.__accessor__ in a later release
                    accessor=self.get_attribute or self.__accessor__,
                    dict_class=self.dict_class,
                    index_errors=self.opts.index_errors,
                    **kwargs
                )

And then looking at marshalling.py line 96:

def serialize(self, obj, fields_dict, many=False,
                  accessor=None, dict_class=dict, index_errors=True, index=None)

I find that the serialize() does not have a **kwargs argument. Putting one in removes the error.

Two questions:

    - What is the purpose of **kwargs in dump()?
    - Should there be an extra argument in the serialize() function?

issue_title: Model validation and unspecified values
issue_body:
Suppose the follwing input. 

    (set-option :rewriter.hi_div0 false)    
    (declare-fun x () (_ BitVec 32))
    (assert (= x (bvudiv (_ bv1 32) (_ bv0 32))))
    ; (push)
    (check-sat)
    (get-value(x))

Currently this produces

    ((x (bvudiv0 #x00000001)))

and all is well. Now switch to incremental mode by uncommenting the `(push)`. It then produces

    ((x #xffffffff))

and the model validation (`model_validate=true`) fails. Adding 

    (set-option :old_simplify.bv.hi_div0 false)

doesn't fix it either, but it does change the result to 

    ((x #x00000000))

and again, model validation fails.

issue_title: Incorrect model involving array and quantifiers
issue_body:
Hi, I'm currently working on a testing tool that makes use of Z3 to generate models that respect several constraints.
I found that when trying to generate models that contain arrays (of Int to any sort) Z3 fails to provide a model, returning as status UNKNOWN and giving as the reason "incomplete quantifiers" however, it seems to generate a model almost correct, with just a slight error.

For instance, imagine the scenario in which I want to generate a model that contains an array of integers in increasing order of length at least 4 and whose elements are greater than 0, smaller than 40 and multiple of 8. When looking on these constraints we see there is just one possible solution (i.e. [8, 16, 24, 32]).

The code I have is the following:

```
(set-option :produce-proofs true)

(declare-datatypes () ((ArrayWithLength.Int (ArrayWithLength.Int_IT (items (Array Int Int)) (length Int)))))
(declare-datatypes () ((Test (Test_IT (array_integer ArrayWithLength.Int)))))
(declare-fun R.request.body () Test)
(assert (let ((a!1 (forall ((i Int))
             (let ((a!1 (and (< 0 i)
                             (< i (length (array_integer R.request.body)))))
                   (a!2 (< (select (items (array_integer R.request.body))
                                   (- i 1))
                           (select (items (array_integer R.request.body)) i))))
               (and (=> a!1 a!2) (>= (length (array_integer R.request.body)) 0)))))
      (a!2 (forall ((__i_1__ Int))
             (let ((a!1 (and (<= 0 __i_1__)
                             (< __i_1__ (length (array_integer R.request.body)))))
                   (a!2 (> (select (items (array_integer R.request.body))
                                   __i_1__)
                           0))
                   (a!3 (< (select (items (array_integer R.request.body))
                                   __i_1__)
                           40))
                   (a!4 (mod (select (items (array_integer R.request.body))
                                     __i_1__)
                             8)))
               (=> a!1 (and a!2 a!3 (= a!4 0)))))))
  (and (>= (length (array_integer R.request.body)) 4) a!1 a!2)))

(check-sat)
(get-info :reason-unknown)
(get-model)
```

(set-option :produce-proofs true) is there so when running in command line Z3 returns a model with (get-model).

ArrayWithLength.Int is a datatype I created so I can control the length of the array, I know Array is an infinite structure but in this way when reading the generated model I know the array is of a specific length (I'm considering indexing starts at 0).


When running that in command line Z3 returns:

```
 unknown
 (:reason-unknown (incomplete quantifiers))
 (model 
   (define-fun R.request.body () Test
     (Test_IT (ArrayWithLength.Int_IT (_ as-array k!4999) 4)))
   (define-fun k!4999!5001 ((x!0 Int)) Int
     (ite (= x!0 2) 24
     (ite (= x!0 1) 16
     (ite (= x!0 0) 8
     (ite (= x!0 3) 32
       24)))))
   (define-fun k!5000 ((x!0 Int)) Int
     (ite (>= x!0 1) (ite (>= x!0 3) 3 1) 0))
   (define-fun k!4999 ((x!0 Int)) Int
     (k!4999!5001 (k!5000 x!0)))
```

When looking in the generated model k!4999!5001 seems absolutely correct (the important indexes for me 0 to 3 map to the expected integers, other indexes return to 24, that is no problem for me, I just ignore all those indexes outside 0 to 3).
The problem is k!5000 which is used to map the index I provide when I want to obtain the value at a specific index into a different index, that is no problem, but (ite (>= x!0 3) 3 1) is the problem, it is saying that all indexes greater than or equal to 3 will just point to 3 (absolutely ok) but the else says that all indexes between [1, 3[ will point into 1 (e.g. the function will make 2 map to 1) which in this scenario would return me the array [8, 16, 16, 32] which is incorrect.

Please note that the first code I generated using the Java API of Z3.

Note: I'm using the commit cb2d8d21071b9e895158adc4b0a3e8dd052e41a4 of master branch.

May you provide some feedback about this? Am I doing something wrong or is it a bug of Z3?

issue_title: Assertion violation in sat_clause_use_list.h with the theory of floating point
issue_body:
Running on `master` at commit `ec5a4ba63dd7caf9894871be5b8e58b61f7109a6`, under a debug + tracing build.  Consider the following input:

(set-logic QF_FP)

issue_title: Problem understanding z3 behaviour with floats
issue_body:
Hello,

I am experimenting with z3 float support. Currently z3 is behaving
erratically, proving some hard VCs and not proving simpler ones. I
have isolated a representative problem to try to understand it with
you.

On this example pasted below, I had to kill z3 (git version) after
~550s. However if I remove the (apparently unrelated) first assertion,
line 9, on "to_bv", z3 returns unsat in less than 2 seconds. Now if I 
add the assertion commented out, line 16, z3 returns unsat in ~90s.

Is this expected ?
Is there any way to anticipate / prevent this behaviour (are the
assertions stated in a bad way, or maybe the trigger's patterns are ...) ?

Thank you,
Clément Fumex
___________________________________________________________

(set-info :source |VC generated by SPARK 2014|)
(set-info :smt-lib-version 2.0)
(set-info :category industrial)
(set-info :status unknown)

(declare-fun to_bv (Float32) (_ BitVec 32))

;; \/ x1 : bv32. is_finite (of_bv x1) -> to_bv (of_bv x1) = x1
(assert
 (forall ((x1 (_ BitVec 32)))
 (! (=>
     (not (or (fp.isInfinite ((_ to_fp 8 24) x1)) (fp.isNaN ((_ to_fp 8 24) x1))))
     (= (to_bv ((_ to_fp 8 24) x1)) x1))
    :pattern ((to_bv ((_ to_fp 8 24) x1))) )))

;; (assert
;;   (forall ((x1 Float32))
;;   (! (=> (not (or (fp.isInfinite x1) (fp.isNaN x1)))
;;          (= ((_ to_fp 8 24) (to_bv x1)) x1)) :pattern (((_ to_fp 8 24) (to_bv x1))) )))

(define-fun l2 () Float32 ((_ to_fp 8 24) #x3F800000)) ; 1.0
(define-fun l3 () Float32 ((_ to_fp 8 24) #x3E800000)) ; 0.25
(define-fun l4 () Float32 ((_ to_fp 8 24) #x454CCCCD)) ; 2 ** 16 / 20 ~= 3276.8
(define-fun l5 () Float32 ((_ to_fp 8 24) #x41200000)) ; 10.0
(define-fun l6 () Float32 ((_ to_fp 8 24) #x41A00000)) ; 20.0

(declare-fun x () (_ BitVec 16))
(declare-fun y () Float32)
(declare-fun res4 () Float32)
(declare-fun res5 () Float32)

(assert (and (fp.leq l3 y) (fp.leq y l2)))                        ; 0.25 <= y <= 1.0
(assert (= res4 (fp.div RNE ((_ to_fp_unsigned 8 24) RNE x) l4))) ; res4 = (float)x / l4
(assert (=> (fp.geq res4 l5) (= res5 (fp.sub RNE res4 l6))))      ; res4 >= 10.0 -> res5 = res4 - 20.0
(assert (=> (not (fp.geq res4 l5)) (= res5 res4)))                ; res4 <  10.0 -> res5 = res4

(assert                                                           ; res5 / y is not finite
 (or (fp.isInfinite (fp.div RNE res5 y))
     (fp.isNaN (fp.div RNE res5 y))))
(check-sat)

issue_title: Wipe Surveyors from the face of the earth.
issue_body:
Surveyors are long out of date. ~~PathGroups~~ SimulationManagers are the future. With the great power of SimulationManagers comes the great ~~responsibility~~ flexibility of ExplorationTechniques.

The last bastion of Surveyor power is the Slicecutor, which executes a provided slice. There is nothing (but lack of humanpower) stopping this from becoming an ExplorationTechnique, and we should a) do that and b) remove Surveyors for good.

issue_body:
Hey, I've written an [mpc-like](https://github.com/wildefyr/mpvc) control script for use with mpv. It uses mpv's `--input-unix-socket` to send commands to an mpv instance started with it. I've so far attempted putting `--input-unix-socket` into playerargs but this seems to have no effect at all. 

The other option I have tried is setting player to my script `mpvc` and playerargs to `add` which adds the the arg given by mps-youtube (a really long URL, given by google's API). The issue with this is that mps-youtube or the API fails to send me a URL at all in most videos I have tried it on. This is especially annoying as mpv can take a normal youtube URL and play it just fine.

A solution to either one would be a dream as I could use mps-youtube purely as a frontend to adding tracks, then closing it completely while keeping the mpv instance it starts running.

issue_title: Enum choice order and input type not working
issue_body:
I detected various more and less serious issues with current Enum implementation. I place them here in one issue as I think they are closely related. Should I split it, maybe?

Currently, if any field converted from django Model to graphene.Field has choices defined, the field is converted to graphene.Enum with corresponding value-label pairs.

**1) Choice order**

Django choices (value, label) order is different from python Enum (label, value) underlying graphene.Enum. Tests have passed as they contained only (string, string) case. While (string, int) throws no exception, (int, string), required by Django field choices, does:

```python
from graphene.contrib.django import DjangoNode

class OneFieldNode(DjangoNode):
    class Meta:
        model = OneFieldModel
```

```python
from django.db import models

class OneFieldModel(models.Model):
    field = models.IntegerField(u'Poziom stanowiska',
                                choices=[(1, u'First choice'), (2, u'Second choice')])
```



```
class OneFieldNode(DjangoNode):
  File "/home/tomek/htdocs/isivi/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/graphene/core/classtypes/base.py", line 31, in __new__
    return mcs.construct(new_class, bases, attrs)
  File "/home/tomek/htdocs/isivi/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/graphene/contrib/django/types.py", line 45, in construct
    cls.construct_fields()
  File "/home/tomek/htdocs/isivi/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/graphene/contrib/django/types.py", line 32, in construct_fields
    converted_field = convert_django_field_with_choices(field)
  File "/home/tomek/htdocs/isivi/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/graphene/contrib/django/converter.py", line 16, in convert_django_field_with_choices
    return Enum(name.upper(), choices, description=field.help_text)
  File "/home/tomek/htdocs/isivi/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/graphene/core/classtypes/enum.py", line 22, in __call__
    '__enum__': PyEnum(name, names)
  File "/home/tomek/htdocs/isivi/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/enum/__init__.py", line 332, in __call__
    return cls._create_(value, names, module=module, type=type)
  File "/home/tomek/htdocs/isivi/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/enum/__init__.py", line 442, in _create_
    classdict[member_name] = member_value
  File "/home/tomek/htdocs/isivi/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/enum/__init__.py", line 123, in __setitem__
    if _is_sunder(key):
  File "/home/tomek/htdocs/isivi/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/enum/__init__.py", line 80, in _is_sunder
    return (name[0] == name[-1] == '_' and 
TypeError: Error when calling the metaclass bases
    'int' object has no attribute '__getitem__'
```

**2) contribute_to_class**
Even when the order issue is fixed, fields converted in this way won't be included in the schema as graphene.Enum class, which is a ClassType (unlike all other FieldType types, into which Django fields are converted), doesn't implement contribute_to_class.

My quick fix for this was (in contrib.django.converter):
```return Field(Enum(name.upper(), choices, description=field.help_text))```
instead of
```return Enum(name.upper(), choices, description=field.help_text)```


**3) Optional conversion**
Finally, automatic conversion to Enum might be optional, as I, for example, have used Django field choices as a kind of label used in frontend, which means that it is not a strict constant name and typically contains spaces. Unfortunately, spaces used in constant names are not supported in python Enum. Thus, obligatory conversion to Enum makes me unable to use my pre-GraphQL Django schema.


**4) Enum as InputType**
The implementation of Enum is still incomplete, as it cannot be used as InputType which is a major drawback. For me, using constant names in relay code would seem to be the most important advantage of graphQL Enum over simple Integer. Below an example that is visibly not implemented
```python
class MyEnum(graphene.Enum):
    FIRST = 1
    SECOND = 2

class OneFieldMutation(ClientIDMutation):
    class Input:
        one = MyEnum # not working
        two = Field(MyEnum) # not working


    @classmethod
    def mutate_and_get_payload(cls, input, info):
        pass
```

issue_title: Modify the TensorFlow Scheduler and Runtime to Change the Operations Priority
issue_body:
I am trying to modify the TensorFlow scheduler and runtime to change the operation priorities. As my understanding, TensorFlow has inter-operation and intra-operation thread pool with a scheduler scheduling operations for different threads and there is also a FIFO queue of operations for operations waiting. The workflow between them is that operations are sent to the inter-op thread pool from the executor, and then that work is running through XLA compiler to eventually be executed on the intra-op thread pool. Schedule() is in inside a specific ThreadPool and is to schedule function for the threads in the pool. The thread scheduler selects a subset of threads to run at any given moment. When the tasks are passed to the ThreadPool, they are added to one of the scheduler thread’s FIFO queues and then the scheduler will pick up the tasks distributed to the available worker threads. I have all the control and data dependencies got from the Graph in TensorFlow by using TensorBoard. Now I am not sure whether my understanding for the TensorFlow scheduler and runtime is correct or not.

The problem is that I still have no clue how and where to modify the threadpool/scheduler/ready_queue or others to change the operations priority/sequence for different threads at the TensorFlow runtime by modifying the source code. Does anyone have any ideas?

issue_title: Plugin design: Do inference to visualize network activations

I wonder, because it seems that one should rather write summaries, that are then processed by the backend, but if one wants to interactively choose inputs, that does not seem appropriate. I am asking, because I currently work on a tool simliar to Yosinski's and integration into Tensorboard would be nice, but I am not sure if this complies with the intended design.

issue_title: AttributeError: Requirement instance has no attribute 'name'
issue_body:
* safety version: 1.1.1
* Python version: 2.7
* Operating System: RHEL7

### Description

Whenever **safety** stumbles over unpinned requirements, it fails with an exception:

This is the result:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/bin/safety", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(cli())
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click/core.py", line 722, in __call__
    return self.main(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click/core.py", line 697, in main
    rv = self.invoke(ctx)
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click/core.py", line 1066, in invoke
    return _process_result(sub_ctx.command.invoke(sub_ctx))
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click/core.py", line 895, in invoke
    return ctx.invoke(self.callback, **ctx.params)
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/click/core.py", line 535, in invoke
    return callback(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/safety/cli.py", line 40, in check
    vulns = safety.check(packages=packages, key=key, db_mirror=db, cached=cache)
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/safety/safety.py", line 130, in check
    for pkg in packages:
  File "/opt/virtualenv/safety/lib/python2.7/site-packages/safety/util.py", line 81, in read_requirements
    req=req.name),
AttributeError: Requirement instance has no attribute 'name'
```

This happens both with unpinned versions in `requirements.txt` and with unpinned versions in the `pip freeze` output (e.g. git snapshots).

In my setup the parsed requirement `req`, which is accessed on line 81 in `utils.py` only has the following attributes:

```
['specifier', 'project_name', 'unsafe_name', 'key', 'hashCmp', '_Requirement__hash', 'specs', 'extras']
```

It looks like you are trying to access the `name` attribute of a `Requirement`, which should not be present according to [setuptool's documentation](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pkg_resources.html#requirement-methods-and-attributes).

issue_title: reunification process
issue_body:
This issue tracks the progress of the reunification process. `STATUS` below will be updated to inform of steps carried out.

For more details on the plan visit: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1wxst7fuBxKxRP6mhN6MGxmyUn9inkn5HoXihZ9xpmf8/edit?usp=sharing

# How to help?

You can help by testing, reviewing the changes and informing of anything that might be broken. Anything you might highlight will be welcomed.

Feel free to add a comment below for general comments or in the following PR if you want to comment on a particular bit of code: https://github.com/napalm-automation/napalm/pull/422

## How can I install the branch so I can test?

### from a cloned repo

```
$ git clone git@github.com:napalm-automation/napalm.git
$ cd napalm
$ git checkout reunification
$ pip install -e .
```

### from github

```
pip install -e git://github.com/napalm-automation/napalm.git@reunification#egg=napalm_reunification
```

### from pip

Not yet, eventually we will publish beta versions on pip so you can install from there.

# STATUS

* reunification branch is ready for testing
* [doc](https://napalm.readthedocs.io/en/reunification/index.html) generation has been fixed

## Drivers migrated

* eos
* ios
* junos

# Tips

## Adapting import paths

If you have import paths like `napalm_eos` or `napalm_base` you can easily migrate your import paths with the following lines:

```
find path/to/your/code -type f -exec sed -i '' 's/napalm_base/napalm\.base/g' {} \;
find path/to/your/code -type f -exec sed -i '' 's/napalm_eos/napalm\.eos/g' {} \;
```

issue_title: Do not globally enable logger.INFO messages
issue_body:
server.py enables INFO messages for the root logger:

`logging.getLogger().setLevel(logging.INFO)
`
This, in turn, enables INFO messages for every included module.  
This is super-annoying because every call to Visdom prints tornado messages to STDOUT:

```
INFO:tornado.access:200 POST /update (::1) 1.03ms
INFO:tornado.access:200 POST /update (::1) 1.03ms
INFO:tornado.access:200 POST /update (::1) 1.95ms
INFO:tornado.access:200 POST /update (::1) 1.01ms
```
Because server.py is a separate process, users can't even disable the logging from our code; we have to modify server.py.

Visdom should probably take logger level from the command line args or a config file, and default to silence.

issue_title: Not clear how to expose existing C++ vector as numpy array
import errors not handled?
Invalid error handling.
issue_body:
# Summary
 1. Wrong approach for exception message updating.
 2. Useless error handling for invalid requests.
 3. Missing response handler for mult-part form request.

## Part 1
At the current moment implemented wrong approach for updating error massage like bellow:
```python
try:
    return json.loads(self.raw)["id"]
except KeyError as error:
    error.message += "Raw: {0}".format(self.raw)
    raise
```
Let's create a simple example `err.py` and test it on different versions of python:
```python
try:
    x = {"abc":123}["id"]
except KeyError as error:
    error.message += "Raw: {0}".format("some additional info")
    raise
```
Here is the output:
```bash
$ python2 err.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "err.py", line 2, in <module>
    x = {"abc":123}["id"]
KeyError: 'id'
```
```bash
$ python3 err.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "err.py", line 2, in <module>
    x = {"abc":123}["id"]
KeyError: 'id'

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "err.py", line 4, in <module>
    error.message += "Raw: {0}".format("some additiona info")
AttributeError: 'KeyError' object has no attribute 'message'
```
As you can see for `python2` there is no any additional info appeared. Moreover, `python3` exception does not have attribute with name `message` (chain exception).

## Part 2 (more serious)
__No exception appeared in case of invalid request!__
Let me show an example [err2.py.txt](https://github.com/reportportal/client-Python/files/999175/err2.py.txt)
Create save log request without time field like bellow:
```python
log_rq = SaveLogRQ(item_id=test.id,
                                   # time=timestamp(),
                                   message="Hello World!",
                                   level="INFO")
service.log(log_rq)
```
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1073415/26032132/1e81abc2-3894-11e7-8f65-f04fd3969f99.png)

__If you run attached file, no error is appeared and no log message is created in the Report Portal!__

You will get error in case of trying to access `id` from response object for invalid save log request:
```python
log_rq = SaveLogRQ(item_id=test.id,
                                  # time=timestamp(),
                                  message="Hello World!",
                                  level="INFO")
r = service.log(log_rq)
print(r.id)
```
Traceback is following:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "err2.py.txt", line 49, in <module>
    print(r.id)
  File "./client-python/reportportal_client/model/response.py", line 17, in id
    return json.loads(self.raw)["id"]
KeyError: 'id'
```
## Part 3 (improvements)
Need to add response handler for multi-part form request, because json part represented as an `array`.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1073415/26032173/52732824-3895-11e7-9786-2c86b264e212.png)

SSLCertificateFile insert_cert_file_path

issue_title: Support resumeable resizing
issue_body:
It would be lovely if `FilesDataset.resize` looked for individual images rather than the entire directory when resizing. That way if you can resume in the middle if something goes wrong in the middle of a large resize process. Could make it a flag eg. `resume=True`

An untested draft of the change:

```python
def resize_imgs(fnames, targ, path, new_path, resume=False):
    """
    Enlarge or shrink a set of images in the same directory to scale, such that the smaller of the height or width dimension is equal to targ.
    Note: 
    -- This function is multithreaded for efficiency. 
    -- When destination file or folder already exist, function exists without raising an error. 
    """
    def do_resize(x, targ, path, new_path):
        if os.path.exists(new_path):
            return
        else:
            resize_img(x, targ, path, new_path)

    if not os.path.exists(os.path.join(path,new_path,str(targ),fnames[0])) or resume:
        with ThreadPoolExecutor(8) as e:
            ims = e.map(lambda x: do_resize(x, targ, path, new_path), fnames)
            for x in tqdm(ims, total=len(fnames), leave=False): pass
    return os.path.join(path,new_path,str(targ))
```

issue_title: Support multiple instances of the same package/provider
issue_body:
Currently, we do not support deploying resources to two different AWS accounts, two Kubernetes clusters, etc. from the same Pulumi program.  This is a requirement for many practical applications of Pulumi.

To support this, we'll need to have a way to create additional instances of a package/provider - such as:

```
import * as aws from "@pulumi/aws"

let usEast1Aws = aws.New({ ... config options ... });
let usWest1Aws = aws.New({  ... config options ...});
let instance = new usEast1Aws.ec2.Instance(...);
```

This poses a few questions:
1. How does this interact with the config system?  Is ambient config always applied only to the "default" instance?
2. How do these separate instances manage their identity across the language<->engine<->provider boundary?  Specifically, how do RPC calls track which instance of the resource provider they should be referring to?
2. Is this a convention-based model that each package or provider can choose how to expose to users, or something that is formalized by `@pulumi/pulumi`?
3. How is TypeScript typing handled for these dynamically created instances of the module?

issue_title: [rllib] Usability improvements
issue_body:
### Describe the problem

This is a placeholder for potential rllib usability improvements:
- Add accessor methods for reading and updating policy / preprocessor state at runtime. This allows more complex training workflows to be expressed in RLlib.
- Support user defined policies (which should be just tensorflow models that define action and loss output tensors). This makes algorithm development possible without needing to modify RLlib source code.
    - Refactor algorithms to match.
- Refactor policy evaluators to be generic parallel samplers that are not coupled with individual algorithms. This goes with the user defined policy change.
    - Support vectorized policy evaluation for performance.
- More general support for multiagent:
   - Support multiple policies in the same environment. The policies may be completely independent. Alternatively, a multi-agent policy could control multiple agents in the environment.
   - Support multiple policy optimizers operating over the same set of policy evaluators. For example, one policy may be trained with DQN, another with A3C.

issue_title: Cannot open file in read-only mode without write permission
issue_body:
When we try to open a read-only file (no write permission) with mode="r", it is changed internally to "r+" before trying to open the file on[ laspy.base.DataProvider.open()](https://github.com/laspy/laspy/blob/master/laspy/base.py#L122).

Mode "r+" expects write permission, so the open fails, and laspy stops with the exception raised on [laspy/base.py, line 128](https://github.com/laspy/laspy/blob/master/laspy/base.py#L128)

Problem found on laspy 1.5.0.

My latest attempt was to create a dummybutton, `dummybutton = Button(description="")` and use pythons `copy` to create a bunch of them. Each one has a new memory position.

issue_title: Ingestor ignores `resampling_method` configuration option
issue_body:
Pretty sure culprit is this pull request:

https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/pull/502/files

Even before that change `Measurement` class didn't have `resampling_method` property, but before that change ingestor used naked dictionaries, so it was ok as far as ingestor goes.

- Easy fix is to whitelist `resampling_method` key in the `Measurement` class.
- Proper fix is to allow per band configuration during load (#548) and switch ingestor to use that instead of injecting `resampling_method` parameter into measurement dict.

    def http_request(self, verb, path, query_data={}, post_data={},
                     streamed=False, files=None, **kwargs):
        """Make an HTTP request to the Gitlab server.

            Args:
                verb (str): The HTTP method to call ('get', 'post', 'put',
                            'delete')
                path (str): Path or full URL to query ('/projects' or
                            'http://whatever/v4/api/projecs')
                query_data (dict): Data to send as query parameters
                post_data (dict): Data to send in the body (will be converted to
                                  json)
                streamed (bool): Whether the data should be streamed
                **kwargs: Extra data to make the query (e.g. sudo, per_page, page)

            Returns:
                A requests result object.

            Raises:
                GitlabHttpError: When the return code is not 2xx
            """

        def sanitized_url(url):
            parsed = six.moves.urllib.parse.urlparse(url)
            new_path = parsed.path.replace('.', '%2E')
            return parsed._replace(path=new_path).geturl()

        url = self._build_url(path)
        params = query_data.copy()
        params.update(kwargs)
        opts = self._get_session_opts(content_type='application/json')

        # don't set the content-type header when uploading files
        if files is not None:
            del opts["headers"]["Content-type"]

        verify = opts.pop('verify')
        timeout = opts.pop('timeout')

        # Requests assumes that `.` should not be encoded as %2E and will make
        # changes to urls using this encoding. Using a prepped request we can
        # get the desired behavior.
        # The Requests behavior is right but it seems that web servers don't
        # always agree with this decision (this is the case with a default
        # gitlab installation)
        req = requests.Request(verb, url, json=post_data, params=params,
                               files=files, **opts)
        prepped = self.session.prepare_request(req)
        prepped.url = sanitized_url(prepped.url)
        result = self.session.send(prepped, stream=streamed, verify=verify,
                                   timeout=timeout)

        if 200 <= result.status_code < 300:
            return result

        try:

        except (KeyError, ValueError, TypeError):
            error_message = result.content

        if result.status_code == 401:
            raise GitlabAuthenticationError(response_code=result.status_code,
                                            error_message=error_message,
                                            response_body=result.content)

        raise GitlabHttpError(response_code=result.status_code,
                              error_message=error_message,

issue_title: pyinstaller / cxfreeze issue in __inti__.py
issue_body:
## Description of the problem, including code/CLI snippet
If you use this module in a project and try to compile it into an exe using pyinstaller or cxfreeze there is a dependency issue. In __init__.py on line 104 there is a dynamic module load which is not detected. As a workaround I was able to add the below line on line 36 so that it was explicitly loaded and able to be found by pyinstaller. 

"""
import gitlab.v4.objects
"""

Since v3 is depricated this change or something more appropriate could probably be put in the code to prevent further trouble for folks building using those tools.

issue_title: Separate caching from compilation in CasADi backend
issue_body: With the newly added support for picklable SXFunctions (and in the future maybe also MXFunctions), there is a proper cache that does not lose any flexibility. The current caching uses CasADi codegen and compilation, which means that we cannot call e.g. `ca.substitute` or` .expand()` anymore on it later, which are things that can significantly simplify the graph and therefore speed of calculations.

issue_title: `raise_from_error` doesn't always raise from error
issue_body:
Over at https://github.com/gratipay/gratipay.com/issues/2869 we're seeing that [`raise_from_error`](https://github.com/idan/oauthlib/blob/04631866c058defb480462db1ec13cb7f88780a9/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/errors.py#L249-L259) sometimes doesn't raise from the error. Here's the `params` in question:

```
{'error': {'type': u'OAuthException', 'message': u'This authorization code has been used.', 'code': 100}}
```

It looks like `raise_from_error` expects `error` to be a simple string, not a dictionary. How did it get to be a dictionary? One clue: we're seeing this when interacting with Facebook. Please let me know what other information might be helpful and I'll try to provide it.

Zooming out: I would expect `raise_from_error` never to not raise. It should always raise. I would expect a fallback exception, maybe something like `UnknownOAuth2Error`, if no other `OAuth2Error` matches.

P.S. As an aside (I can reticket separately if you like), `params.get` on [line 253](https://github.com/idan/oauthlib/blob/04631866c058defb480462db1ec13cb7f88780a9/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/errors.py#L253) will fail with an `AttributeError` if `params` is `None`, which is the default value for that function parameter.

issue_title: RLBot breaks if human players are not defined first in the cfg
issue_body:
In rlbot.cfg, you can define some players as 'human' (as opposed to rlbot, etc). Currently, all human players (and probably party_member_bots) MUST come before any other player types in the list. Otherwise, the indices will be messed up and all kinds of chaos happens.

This is probably because the game has a concept of a human index, and we do not keep it properly distinct from our own player indices.
